narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in competitive fields like this, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—things like their early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPAs, family income levels, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it estimated a 47.9% probability of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that triggered the rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position, signaling to the model a potential struggle in advanced coursework that often correlates with bar success. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 36 was close to the average of 36.7 and strongly pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass, showing your aptitude for legal reasoning stands out. Your upper-middle family income also helped, nudging things positively since students from similar backgrounds often have more resources to succeed. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, a bit below the 3.21 average, and your Year 1 decile of 2 both offered some counterbalance toward passing but weren't strong enough to offset the top concern. Planning full-time studies aligned with the vast majority of students and provided a small positive lift too. Compared to typical applicants who pass, your profile is solid in test scores and background but held back by those lower decile rankings, which the model sees as the strongest signal of bar risk. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted effort. Next, I recommend focusing on boosting your academic deciles by seeking tutoring or study groups now, retaking the LSAT if possible to push even higher, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience as a Black woman in law. Reapply after addressing these, perhaps to schools with holistic reviews, and consider bar prep resources early—you've got the drive to turn this around. We're rooting for you.
